ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A003K covers cola-type soft drinks generically; no dedicated reduced-sugar sub-code is available. closest_parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for reduced-sugar cola beverage; null applied to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single-serve can of 330 ml, equivalent to approximately 330 g, is the most common portion consumed at one sitting.

nutrient_source.title: Cola drink, less sugar — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id D06020008 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. Values are accepted as exact match.

gi_source.title: Soft drink, cola-type — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 65 (medium) is drawn from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cola-type carbonated soft drinks. The less-sugar variant has not been independently tested in a published GI study; the GI of the sugar fraction (sucrose) remains approximately 65. Glycemic load per 330 ml serving calculated as (65 × 25.5) / 100 = 16.6, rounded to 17.0, where 25.5 g carbohydrate per serving = 7.72 g/100 g × 3.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.72 g sugar per 100 ml; liquid sugar independently raises blood pressure risk regardless of low sodium content (3 mg/100 ml)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage; the 7.72 g/100 ml sugar content exceeds the 5 g/100 ml SSB threshold and has been independently associated with elevated blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidance — choose zero-sugar cola or plain sparkling water instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (7.72 g sugar/100 ml, zero dietary fibre) causing rapid postprandial glucose absorption; GI 65 with glycemic load of 17 per standard 330 ml can
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this drink; despite the less-sugar label it remains a sugar-sweetened beverage that causes rapid blood glucose spikes — substitute with zero-sugar cola, unsweetened sparkling water, or plain water.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased triglycerides, visceral adiposity, and cardiovascular risk; phosphoric acid may impair calcium absorption relevant to vascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit or eliminate this beverage; habitual SSB consumption raises triglycerides and promotes visceral fat accumulation — replace with water, unsweetened herbal tea, or zero-sugar alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar at 7.72 g/100 ml promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; phosphoric acid may impair calcium absorption relevant to RA bone health; no anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage as its sugar content promotes systemic inflammation and phosphoric acid may reduce calcium absorption — choose anti-inflammatory beverages such as green tea or plain water instead.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cola Drink (Less Sugar) is a commercially manufactured, carbonated soft drink formulated with a reduced sugar content compared to standard full-sugar cola. It delivers the characteristic cola flavour — a blend of caramel, vanilla, citrus, and spice notes derived from natural flavourings — in a lightly sweet, effervescent format. The beverage contains 7.72 g of sugar per 100 ml, which is lower than a standard cola (approximately 10–11 g/100 ml) but still qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under Singapore HPB and WHO definitions. It provides 31 kcal per 100 ml, with negligible protein and fat. Dietary fibre is absent. Sodium is very low at 3 mg/100 ml. The glycemic index is approximately 65 (medium), and a standard 330 ml can delivers a glycemic load of approximately 17, making it a meaningful glucose challenge for people with diabetes or insulin resistance. Clinically, this product is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its liquid sugar content, which is absorbed rapidly without the buffering effect of fibre. The phosphoric acid content may impair calcium absorption. Despite the less-sugar positioning, this product should not be interpreted as a health-positive choice. It is ultra-processed, contains no micronutrients of significance, and provides empty calories. Suitable alternatives include zero-sugar cola, plain sparkling water, or unsweetened beverages.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics drink less sugar cola?
How many calories in a can of less sugar cola?
Is reduced sugar cola OK for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of less sugar cola?
Is less sugar cola better than regular cola?
Cola drink less sugar GI glycemic index

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D06020008, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Exact match to an approved primary regional source. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous cola entry with documented basis.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero by formulation — this is a carbonated sugar-water beverage containing no protein ingredients. fat_g is confirmed zero by formulation — no fat-containing ingredients are present in cola soft drinks. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero by formulation. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero — carbonated soft drinks contain no dietary fibre. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this specific less-sugar cola variant are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo database entry or USDA FoodData Central for this exact formulation as of 2026-04-16. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 in the HPB source and are accepted as confirmed zero. GI value of 65 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cola-type carbonated soft drinks; the less-sugar variant has not been independently tested in a published GI study. Glycemic load per 330 ml serving calculated as (65 × 25.5) / 100 = 16.6, rounded to 17.0. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03301589 and FoodEx2 A003K applied as closest_parent matches; no exact reduced-sugar cola subclass exists in either system.
